SHRI LAVU SRIKRISHNA DEVARAYALU (NARASARAOPET): Sir, last time,
when we had the Interim Budget, a lot of people were saying that NDA Government
would not work. But now we are seeing INDIA Alliance crumbling and they are
fighting amongst themselves. It is a good change to see in eight months. Also, it is
refreshing to see there is no external influence in this Session. There is no Soros,
there is no Hindenburg, and there is no Pegasus. A narrative is being set here.
I want to congratulate our hon. Finance Minister. It is her eighth Budget in a
row. It is a big feat. India is still one of the fastest growing economies. It is a major
economy. We should remember that. We have achieved 4.7 per cent fiscal deficit.
We are aiming at 4.4 per cent fiscal deficit in the next one year or so. Our debt to
GDP ratio is also 57 per cent of the GDP. In comparison, the US has 1.3 per cent,
France has 1.9 per cent and Germany has 62 per cent. We are doing very well in
this field. India is still doing very well in banking. We have the least number of NPAs
in the Indian banking sector. So, we should remember this and we should
10.02.2025 1059
congratulate the hon. Finance Minister. I want to thank the hon. Finance Minister
on behalf of the middle class and the taxpayers who are being given an opportunity
of not paying any income tax up to the income of Rs. 12 lakh. I want to thank the
hon. Finance Minister for the enhanced allocation to the PLI Scheme. The
allocation has been enhanced from Rs. 9,300 crore to Rs. 19,482 crore. It is almost
a jump of 110 per cent enhancement in the PLI Scheme. There is a lot of comments
that are coming from the Opposition side saying that PLI Scheme has been used
not for the manufacturing but only for assembling. I have seen it in my own State
where the PLI Scheme has been availed by food processing industries and they
are doing fantastically well for the last two to three years. The Opposition Members
spoke so much about the iPhones that only assembly of iPhones is happening in
India but not the manufacturing. If you go by the data, when the PLI Scheme was
announced for iPhones, there was only four per cent indigenisation. But now, there
is 30 per cent indigenisation. It takes time. It is growing and the industries are
developing around the iPhone manufacturing. If my numbers are wrong, please
correct me.
I also want to thank the Finance Minister on behalf of Andhra Pradesh
because she has considerately allocated Rs.15,000 crore for development of
Amaravati which is our State capital, and which was lying stagnant from 2019 to
2024. She has given a new lease of life to Amaravati. Rs. 12,000 crore have been
sanctioned for Polavaram Irrigation Project. It is a lifeline of Andhra Pradesh. With
this project, millions of acres of land can be irrigated; millions of homes can be
given drinking water. I thank her for that. Rs. 11,000 crore have been sanctioned
10.02.2025 1060
for restructuring of steel plant at Visakhapatnam. This is the pride of Andhra
Pradesh and I am really thankful to the Finance Minister for the respect that she
has shown for the people of Andhra Pradesh. Rs. 4,300 crore have been given for
Kopparthy and Orvakal Industrial Corridors. This will create a lot of employment
and bring in a lot of investment in Rayalaseema which is a backward area.
I also want to thank the Railway Minister for laying the foundation of Railway
Zone headquarters at Visakhapatnam. On behalf of the industries from Andhra
Pradesh, I want to thank the Finance Minister for reducing the BCD on the frozen
fish from 30 per cent to five per cent and removing the BCD on crushed leather and
also on 36 life-saving medicines, including those for cancer and non-cancer
treatment. I thank her for that.
On behalf of all the people of Andhra Pradesh, I thank her. We needed this
encouragement; we needed this drive and push from the Union Government so that
Andhra Pradesh can develop. I have a report with me, Sir. This is a report given by
the NITI Aayog; this is not given by me; this is not given by any political party. It
describes the fiscal health of Andhra Pradesh until 2023. The report has come out
in 2025 but the health is monitored until 2023. As per that, out of 18 large States,
Andhra Pradesh is lying at 17th position. Our revenue growth has come down from
17 per cent in 2018-19 to 9.8 per cent in 2022-23. Public debt has increased at an
average rate of 16.5 per cent during the period of 2018-19 to 2022-23. There is
almost 16.5 per cent increase. From 2014 to 2019, this is really an awful figure.
From 2014 to 2019, when we borrowed Rs.100, we almost spent Rs.59 on capex.
But from 2019 to 2024, when we borrowed Rs.100, we only spent Rs.22 on capex.
10.02.2025 1061
This has really stagnated all the infrastructure projects in Andhra Pradesh. Our
Chief Minister has been talking about it and the results in Andhra Pradesh have
very clearly shown that for any leader, it is not possible to do welfare without
creating any wealth. I know when I talk about it, they will definitely cut this video
and say that Krishna spoke against the welfare.
We are not against the welfarism; but we want welfarism to be done after
wealth creation. As we are doing a debate across the nation, even in Delhi election,
they have shown that welfarism and development has to go together. As the debate
is going on with regard to One Nation, One Election, we want the debate to happen
even on this. Our Chief Minister Chandrababu Naidu is clearly saying that we
should have a debate across the nation that no one has a right to do the welfarism
without creating wealth. I hope the Finance Minister takes it up.
Coming to the departments, with regard to Railways, there is an increase of
Rs.9,400 crore. I thank the Railway Minister for that. I request that the railway line
projects, which are stagnated because of land acquisition issues, should be
expedited. For MGNREGA, there is allocation of Rs.86,000 crore. I know this is
demand-driven and I am sure there will be more funds for this in the Supplementary
Demands for Grants. My only request from Andhra Pradesh is to include handloom
weavers and handicraft workers. They have been saving this field for so many
years. It is a family tradition that they are following.
Coming to agriculture, there is almost 3.99 per cent increase in allocation.
The most heartening thing to see is that in every alternate Cabinet meeting, there
is something that is discussed and some decision made regarding agriculture. So,
10.02.2025 1062
this is a good thing to see. But recently, I was sitting with a few of the farmers from
my constituency. One of them reads very well and remembers very well. So, he
has asked me three pertinent questions. He asked the first question. He said, ‘I
have seen the Central Government employees getting the Sixth Pay Commission,
Seventh Pay Commission and now the Eighth Pay Commission, but I am getting
this PM- Kisan Samman Nidhi of Rs. 6000 from 2018’. He asked, `When they are
getting the Eighth Pay Commission, their salaries are getting increased, why cannot
the amount of Rs. 6000 be increased?’ So, in his own words, he is asking the PM
Kisan Samman Nidhi to be indexed with the inflation.
The second question he has asked was whether tenant farmers can be
brought into the net of PM Kisan. We have enough technology, enough men to
actually get the farmers on board. So, if the tenant farmers can be onboard on PM-
Kisan, that would be a huge favour.
The third and the most important one question is about procurement of
various food products. There are some products that have been procured, but most
of the products are not being procured. Say, for example, in my district, there is
chilli cultivation that happens, but the problem is that it is not in the procurement
list. So, the request from our farmers' side is to include it. They will not ask every
year, but maybe it happens once every 10 years or once every eight years. So,
when the prices really go down, we should have some sort of a mechanism, some
sort of a special fund, so that the Central Government and the State Government
can intervene and stabilize the prices. So, this is the third request from the farmers.
I need clarity with regard to the Fasal Bima Yojana. In 2024-25, a sum of Rs.
10.02.2025 1063
15,000 crore have been allocated, but in 2025-26, it has come down to Rs. 12,000
crore. So, I request for clarity whether this budget will be increased in the
Supplementary Demand for Grants or some sort of a mechanism has been put in
place for this.
Now, I am coming to Education, Jal Shakti and Housing. There are huge
increments in the Budget, but the problem with all these three Ministries is that
there is under-utilisation of funds. In Education, there is 5 per cent under-utilisation;
in Jal Shakti, it is 47 per cent; in Housing, it is 22 per cent, I am sure the reason
being that most of the States which are ruled by Opposition are not coming forward
and actually accessing these funds. It may be because of that. In the State of
Andhra Pradesh, we have a lot of avenues wherein these funds can be utilised
properly. For example, a lot of higher education institutions have been sanctioned
for us. Most of them are running in temporary campuses like IISER and IIIT. So, we
request funding to be done so that this under-utilisation will not happen in
education. Also with regard to PM SHRI schools, we recommended for 2,369, but
only 855 have been selected. We request other schools also to be selected.
Coming to the STARS Programme, there are only six States that have been
selected and I do not know why? A State like Kerala has been selected. They are
doing fantastically well for so many years in school education. Himachal Pradesh,
Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, Odisha and Kerala, these six States
have been selected. We request Andhra to be included in it because as per the
ASER results that have been announced recently, the worst-performing State is
Andhra Pradesh because of the last five years of mis-governance in the education
10.02.2025 1064
system. Even our Education Minister, Nara Lokesh Garu has recently come to
Delhi. He has also requested for this. So, we request Andhra Pradesh to be
included in the STARS Programme.
Coming to Jal Shakti, there is a river-linking project that we are asking for
from the Godavari to Penna. If that can be sanctioned from the Central
Government, this under-utilisation will not happen.
Coming to the Housing and Urban Affairs, again, we have asked for the
metros at Visakhapatnam and Vijayawada. This is under the AP Reorganization
Act. If this can be sanctioned, this under-utilisation will not happen.
Sir, I conclude with just three requests from our side. Just give me two more
minutes. The first request is not just related to Andhra Pradesh, but it is related to
the whole country. There are large businesses that are consolidating like steel,
cement. They come down from ten groups to three groups or four groups. Also,
there is a PLI Scheme that has been given to the various sectors. They are also
doing fine. But the main contention is with regard to the SMEs. The SMEs, which
are the growth engines, which create a lot of employment, are facing huge issues
with regard to high logistics costs, access to technology etc. Just give me two
minutes, I will conclude. They asking for risk capital to be there. What do you mean
by risk capital? Normally, when they have to raise money so that they can improve
their balance sheet, they can get the new technology, and they can make sure that
they can produce better and compete with the others in the world level, they have
to go through the banks. The banks ask for 150 per cent or 200 per cent security.
It is helpful for everyone in the SMEs. So, what I request is this. In 2022, 109 SMEs
10.02.2025 1065
have gone for IPOs.
In 2024, there were 235 SMEs that had gone for IPO. I know that from my
erstwhile State, where Hyderabad was the capital, hundreds of Defence industries
were there with a turnover of Rs.5200 crore. They can double it; they can triple it.
All they need is the risk capital. I remember that they all benefited when there was
a stock exchange that was functioning in Hyderabad. When we have arbitration
centres across the country, when we have regional banks happening across the
country, why can’t we have stock exchanges specially for SMEs at least in the major
cities? This is a request.
I conclude with the last one point with regard to the basic customs duty which
has been reduced on automobiles, electronics, etc. There are almost 51 products
on which BCD has been reduced. But out of 51 products, there are 37 products on
which a cess has been levied. The Finance Minister has mentioned many times in
the Budget also that we will work in partnership with the States. So, let us work
together.
HON. CHAIRPERSON: Kindly conclude.
SHRI LAVU SRIKRISHNA DEVARAYALU: Sir, I am just concluding. So, the
request from our side is that regarding the cess that has been collected, let us work
out some formula so that the States and the Centre can benefit from it. So, this
can be given both to the States and the Centre.
With regard to funding the new technology, the Opposition Members have
mentioned that we have done really well with regard to information technology. But
I think, it is only a road half the way because when you look at TCS and Infosys,
10.02.2025 1066
they have done extremely well in the service sector. But the problem is that if you

renewable energy and new-age mobility, we should not be left behind. We should
not be a service destination. We should be there to be a technology provider. Under
the National Research Foundation, Rs.50,000 crore were announced four years
back. Somehow that money has not trickled down. This year also, Rs.20,000 crore
have been given in the Budget. I hope that the money will trickle down so that new
age technology can be taken forward.
I made all these requests with a positive impatience. This is because the
other day, the Prime Minister had mentioned in his speech on the President’s

also making this suggestion. I want the country to move forward. I want the Viksit
Bharat to happen not in 2047, but much before that.
Thank you very much.

बह(cid:242)त बड़ा योगदान है । आज पंजाब के िकसान(cid:332) को डायविस(cid:170)िफके शन के िलए (cid:214)पेशल बजट िमले तािक
पंजाब के पानी और पंजाब क(cid:236) धरती को बचाया जा सके ।
दूसरा, बड़े दखु क(cid:236) बात है िक इस पूरे बजट म(cid:164), िपछले बजट म(cid:164) भी, पंजाब का नाम तक नह(cid:233)
िलया गया, इस बार भी न पंजाब का और न पंजाब के िकसान(cid:332) का, िपछले तीन साल से पंजाब के

SHRI P. V. MIDHUN REDDY (RAJAMPET): Thank you, Sir, for letting me speak
on this Union Budget.
Sir, the hon. Finance Minister's decision to give income tax Incentives up to
10.02.2025 1078
Rs. 12 lakh is a welcome move. Though there is a reduction of Rs. 1 lakh crore of
tax revenues, I think it will have a ripple effect on the economy, and it will boost the
economy.
Sir, I would like to recall that the budgeted Capex for 2024-25 is Rs.
11,11,111 crore. But if you see the Revised Estimate, it is Rs. 10,18,249 crore
which is a reduction of Rs. 92,000 crore. And, even this year, in spite of a big Budget
which has been announced, the Capex budgeted for this year is Rs. 11,21,090
crore, which is only 0.89 per cent marginal increase than the previous year. If you
see last year, the non-tax revenues basically depended on the revenues from the
RBI, and it was Rs. 2,10,784 crore, which is very unlikely to happen this year. Also,
whatever the Government has planned to raise through disinvestment, they have
achieved only 30 per cent of the target. So, this year, it is going to be very tough to
keep up the Capex. We urge the Government to, at least, borrow more and see
that the Capex is not reduced.
Sir, if you see the Jal Jeevan Mission, the previous budgeted expenses were
Rs. 70,692 crore. But if you see the ground reality, only Rs. 22,694 crore is the
Revised Estimate. So, there is a reduction of Rs. 47,000 crore.
In Andhra Pradesh, they are planning to call tenders to the tune of Rs. 50,000
crore to 60,000 crore throughout the country. They are taking up Jal Jeevan Mission
ambitiously. But if only Rs. 26,000 crore is being spent, how long will it take for the
scheme to be implemented? It is going to take more than 10 years, which is not in
our right interest. We want the Government to spend whatever is budgeted, and it
can even increase the allocation for Jal Jeevan Mission.
10.02.2025 1079
Sir, there is another important issue in our State I would like to talk about.
During the Question Hour, the Finance Minister, in one of her replies, spoke about
Sahara. She said that they collected funds against the RBI norms. She also said
that there are a lot of beneficiaries or depositors who have been unidentified, and
who have diverted the money. They have taken action against that. I totally agree
with her decision. I want to know why Margadarsi is let off. … (Interruptions) They
have collected the deposits worth Rs. 2,600 crore. They have diverted that which
is against the RBI norms. The RBI has filed an affidavit saying that it is completely
against what they have collected. They have collected in cash, and the Income Tax
Department has slapped a notice of Rs. 1,000 crore. They went to the court and
got a stay.
Sir, this is one of the biggest scams. Law should be the same for everybody.
Just because they have a Media House, they should not be let off for scamming
the people. … (Interruptions)
Sir, I have raised this issue every day. ....(Interruptions) Every day, they are
writing news about me. ....(Interruptions) I am not going to yield; I am not going to
budge and I really do not care what they write. We are going to take this issue in a
big way. ....(Interruptions) We will fight it out, and we will see that justice is done.
There are lakhs of depositors who have been duped in this. This is not a proper
thing. We ask the Government to keep their word. Where is the ED? Where is the
Serious Fraud Investigation Office? What is happening to them when such a big
fraud has happened?
I will just tell one thing to all the hon. Members who are present in this House.

SHRI KALYAN BANERJEE (SREERAMPUR): Sir, our country is always beautiful,
developed and developing itself in each year after Independence in terms of
heritage, culture, education, archaeology, mentality and role model of religious
unity.
India is widely considered as one of the best and oldest living civilizations
with evidence of quality life and stable economy in the world dating back to at least
8000 BCE. Who will get the credit of this development and beauty each and every
year? Who will get the credit? The people of the country will get the credit. It is due
 Not recorded.
10.02.2025 1089
to the contribution of the people of this country, India has developed. No single
person can claim that because of him, India has developed. To see whether any
budget in the past few years has fulfilled the expectation of the people, the
immediate test is the stock market’s response. Sensex and Nifty have maintained
a flat benchmark in the stock market for the last 10 days and have not affected the
market growth. Even real stocks, including RVNL, which would have been one of
the biggest beneficial of the capex hike, have seen a significant decline of up to
nine per cent following the Union Budget. This proves that the Budget for 2025-26
has neglected the challenge of the economy and the common demands of the
people. It has not met the expectation of market participants leaving the common
critical issues unresolved. With public debt at nearly 80 per cent of the GDP and
interest payment consuming a quarter of Government revenue, this Budget is
shocking and a fiscal betrayal to the people as well as strategic failure in driving
economic growth. The Budget is anti-people and destructive for India's economy.
The Union Budget 2025-26 has failed to address several key economic
challenges, including currency depreciation and declining markets. But it is a bold
push towards the complete privatization of the economy. In 2014-15, the
Government promoted a composite cap on foreign investment, raising it to 49 per
cent, which has now been replaced by 100 per cent. The question is whether the
entire population will be covered under insurance and medical insurance for
availing treatment facilities at one-rupee premium cost or not. In 2019, the
penetration of both life and non-life insurance in relation to GDP was 3.7 per cent,
and it remains the same in 2024 due to absence of a people-friendly claim and
10.02.2025 1090
policy mechanism.
I have another crucial question regarding FDI in insurance and the
Government's policy on LIC and other public sector insurance companies. How
long will they keep publicity secured and avoid disinvestment process? The GST
has not been reduced in the insurance sector, while 100 per cent foreign investment
is not allowed in the insurance sector. Our Chief Minister, the Chief of the Trinamool
Congress, Mamata Banerjee ji, has been opposing FDI in insurance since 2012,
and she stood against increasing the foreign investment cap in insurance sector to
100 per cent. She expressed her concern over the plight of people's deposits in LIC
and other public sector companies saying that the existence of such institutions
might be at stake in future.
Our hon. Prime Minister always speaks about reform, perform, transform, but
he seems to be acknowledging what his Government has failed to do. The Budget
ignores the immediate challenges of growing unemployment, inflation and
inequality. The labour share of GDP dropped from 49 per cent in 2017 to 45.2 per
cent in 2023. In terms of the 2025-26 Budget and the 2019-20 Budget, we can see
that in 2019-20, borrowing and other liabilities were 20 per cent; they are 24 per
cent now. The non-tax revenue, which was nine per cent, remains the same;
custom duty was four per cent, which remains the same; corporate tax was 21 per
cent, which is reduced to 17 per cent; GST was 19 per cent, which is reduced to 18
per cent; central excise duty was eight per cent, which is reduced to five per cent
now; income-tax was 16 per cent which is now 22 per cent; non-debt capital
receipts were three per cent which are now reduced to one per cent. In 2019-20,
10.02.2025 1091
the interest payment was 18 per cent which is now 20 per cent; expenditure on
centrally-sponsored scheme was nine per cent, which is now eight per cent. In
2019-20, the subsidies were eight per cent, which have gone down to six per cent.
In 2019-20, the defence expenditure was nine per cent, which is eight per cent now;
the expenditure on Finance Commission and transports was seven per cent, which
is eight per cent now; other expenditure was eight per cent, which remains the
same; expenditure on pension was five per cent which is reduced to four per cent;
expenditure on the Centrally-sponsored schemes was 30 per cent, which is 16 per
cent now. The data clearly shows that interest payment but not repayment of foreign
loans, compromises with funding of Centrally-sponsored schemes, subsidies,
pensions etc. The country’s external debt has risen to Rs.60.82 lakh crore in 2025
compared to Rs.39.50 lakh crore in 2019.
A complete Budget in second term was addressed in 2019. Now they are
delivering a complete budget in 2025. See how it has been reduced in their regime
itself. Furthermore, loans and foreign borrowings are proposed to increase by two
per cent, a move towards ridding with a huge debt. Before I go into other arena, I
want to point out that in the recent past, I came to understand that the functioning
of NCLTs and NCLATs are not in line with the objective of Code. The promoters
are delaying the process directly or indirectly by making more litigations in the CIRP
and liquidation proceedings. It is noticed that the NCLTs and NCLATs are not giving
judgements in time. They are reserving the orders for months and months. I would
like to know how many orders are reserved across India as on today, and for how
many days, these orders are pending to be pronounced. What is the message the
10.02.2025 1092
NCLT judges are trying to convey by holding these orders?
Sir, by increasing the income limit for being taxed to Rs. 12 lakh, around one
crore taxpayers, who were previously required to pay tax, will now pay none. The
total expenditure as a proportion of the GDP is projected to decrease from the
current 14.60 per cent to 14.20 per cent. Only 2.84 crore income tax payees will
benefit from the income-tax exemption announced for next year. This is only 22 per
cent of the total number of salaried workers in India and has no relevance to
maximum working-class people of the country. Only 22 per cent of working class
will get this benefit; rest of them will not get this benefit. The allocation to
MGNREGA has been reduced to Rs.86,000 crore from Rs. 89,153 crore in 2023-
24. A lesser allocation in MGNREGA and PM-Kisan scheme indicates stagnation.
What will this budget do? The allocation for social service has been reduced by 16
per cent; housing funds have been cut by 4.38 per cent; and spending on social
welfare and Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes has been reduced by more
than three per cent. Most importantly, the Budget allocation for food subsidies has
been reduced by four per cent. There is nothing for youth, women and farmers in
this Budget. Regarding social sector spending, the total net receipts for the Centre
are estimated at Rs. 28.37 lakh crore while the total expenditure stands at Rs.50.65
lakh crore, signalling continued fiscal constraint as on December 31, 2024.
Sir, now I will speak about the Revised Estimates and the funds released.
For Samagra Shiksha, fund estimated is Rs. 37,010 crore in 2024-25, released Rs.
17,605 crore. For AMRUT, the Revised Estimate was Rs. 6,000 crore, fund
released was Rs. 4,158 crore. For Swachh Bharat Mission (Urban), fund estimated
10.02.2025 1093
is Rs. 2,159 crore, fund released is Rs. 1,346 crore. For Jal Jeevan Mission, it has
been reduced to Rs. 21,871 crore.
With regard to the issue of unemployment, the economy requires 78.5 lakh
new non-farm jobs every year, but there is no clear road map to achieve this. Sir,
as reported by The Economic Survey, average monthly real earnings of self-
employed men dropped from Rs. 9,454 and to Rs. 8,591 in 2023-24. If I read all the
data, even in the agriculture field, everywhere, this is reducing.
Now, I will speak about child development. These are the three important
areas which I will look into. Around 35.5 per cent of children under five years of age
are stunted. The maternal mortality rate is 97 per one lakh live births.
Now, I will be talking about a very important issue which is appealing to my
mind, that is the issue of women’s empowerment. The empowerment of women
depends upon economic and financial independence. If the economic and financial
independence of women are not there, there cannot be any women’s
empowerment. What do we have to do? We have to take pathways to educate all
the girl children of this country. Unless they are educated, women's empowerment
will be an unsuccessful slogan. From my political, professional and personal
experience, I can say that the women of the country are really in danger now.
Today's result is not good. It is everywhere. The concept of marriage is almost
gone. That bonding is not there. Everything is gone. Almost 70 per cent of women
are divorced. They do not know how to maintain their livelihood. We have to do
something for them. There should be an effort by everyone or rather, I will say to
the girls and women of this country, through this House, to first stand up on their
10.02.2025 1094
own feet and then think about marriage later on. Marriage is one of the
requirements of life. I can understand that, but education is a compulsory
requirement. … (Interruptions) I have only spoken for 10 minutes. Sir, it is required.
We have to do it. We have to decide how we will do it. We have to do something
for the women. We request you to kindly do it.
Tourism is a significant boost to the economy. The Budget has allocated
funds for the development of top 50 tourist destinations in partnership with the State
Governments through a `Challenge Mode’, which is a positive approach. However,
after spending over Rs 2,500 crore on the 45-day long Maha Kumbh Mela at
Prayagraj, the Government has not taken a visible action regarding the massive
and unfortunate stampede on 29th January, 2025. There is no clarity on the post-
mortem reports, the number of deaths, compensation for the affected families or
the issuance of death certificates. Why has the Central Government not taken any
action or initiated inquiries into the negligence of the local State Government? You
have made it an internal issue. It is no longer a State issue. You have advertised it
widely. Why has the Kumbh Mela been made an internal issue? Why should there
not be an inquiry by the Central Government itself? Why? That should be done.
One JPC should be constituted by the Parliament to look into the unfortunate
incident that occurred at the Maha Kumbh Mela.
Now, I come to the issue. The hon. Finance Minister has given much benefit
to Bihar. We do not have anything to say. We have no objection. Bihar needs it;
Bihar should get it. But why have you neglected Bengal? That is our question. No
scheme has been introduced for the development of Bengal. Systematically, the
10.02.2025 1095
Central financial benefits have been withdrawn from Bengal for one reason or the
other without any foundation.

was held last week under the leadership of our hon. Chief Minister. A number of
countries, 40-plus, attended the Summit. Some of the countries are Bhutan,
Germany, Japan, Kenya, Malaysia, Morocco, and the United Kingdom. There were
40 countries. There were 16 sectoral sessions on different subjects. There were
six country sessions with Bhutan, Japan, Germany, Poland, Malaysia and the
Netherlands. There was an exhibition at Biswa Bangla Mela Prangan. There were
215 stalls covering key sectors. Top exhibitions were from the Government of
Jharkhand, ITC, RPSG Group, Titagarh Rail Systems, ONGC, Jupiter Wagons,
Patton International, Ambuja Neotia Group, Kolkata Port Trust, Manipal Hospitals,
JIS Group, etc. Coming to the investment and impact, the total investment proposal
that has come during the last two days is Rs.4,40,595 crore. I am not naming one
big industrialist; I am speaking about a very famous, recognised, gentleman
industrialist. I am not taking his name. He has said that Bengal is the place where
the industry will be developed. Today’s industrial development of Bengal is
because of Mamata. That he has said very categorically. We have seen it from
his speech itself. I am not taking his name. He is not here. I am not supposed to
take his name. Therefore, we have done so many things.
We are having a crunch. No financial sanction is given from the Central
Government either in MGNREGA, or in Awas Yojana or in anything. Everything is
being done from the fund of the State Government. That is being done by the hon.
10.02.2025 1096
Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee.
We can say that we do not mind if you ignore us. But for every ignorance of
yours, people of Bengal will give answer every day. You will get the answer in
2026. Very nicely, you will get the answer in 2026 when the BJP will not get even
30 seats, will not get even an Opposition leader. I can assure you that if you neglect
Bengal, people of Bengal will give you an answer. Now, journey will be done every
day. The Prime Minister will go to Bengal. Then, Amit Shah will go. Then, Nirmala
Sitharaman will go. Everybody will go to Bengal every day. But ultimately, they will
not get even 30 seats. You ignore Bengal. The people of Bengal will give you an
answer. BJP will get a big slap.
Thank you, Sir.

जाए ।
महोदय, जो सांसद िनिध है, िजसम(cid:164) सांसद(cid:332) को के वल 5 करोड़ (cid:321)पये िदए जाते ह(cid:167) और उ(cid:176)र
(cid:255)देश के एम.एल.ए. को भी 5 करोड़ (cid:321)पये िदए जाते ह(cid:167) । हमारी मांग है िक सांसद िनिध को बढ़ा कर 25
करोड़ (cid:321)पये िकया जाए या िफर इसे िसर े से खा(cid:229)रज़ िकया जाए ।

10.02.2025 1100
HON. CHAIRPERSON: No, two more members are there in your Party who wants
to speak.
SUSHRI IQRA CHOUDHARY: Sir, I am just concluding.
The Union Budget 2025-26 was an opportunity to address the pressing
concerns of India's rising unemployment, soaring inflation, crumbling public
services and deepening rural distress. Yet, instead of offering real solutions, the
Government has focused on optics and half-measures. While tax relief for the
middle class is welcome, it does nothing for the millions who earn too little to benefit
from it. Education remains underfunded, rural employment stagnates, and farmers
continue to struggle without meaningful support. The delays in conducting the
Census and Caste Census further expose the Government's disregard for data-
driven policymaking and social justice.
India needs a budget that prioritises its people, not just its economic
headlines. A budget that invests in human capital strengthens social security and
ensures dignity and stability for all. But what we have instead is another missed
opportunity, one that fails the poor, the youth, the farmers, and the women of this
country.

ब-े दम ह(cid:242)ए बीमार, दवा (cid:179)यू ँ नह(cid:233) देते,
तुम अ(cid:184)छे मसीहा हो, िशफ़ा (cid:179)यू ँ नह(cid:233) देते,
िमट जायेगी मख़लूक़, तो इंसाफ़ करोगे,
मुंिसफ़ हो तो, अब हशर उठा (cid:179)यू ँ नह(cid:233) देते ।

SHRI SUBBARAYAN K. (TIRUPPUR): Madam Chairperson, Vanakkam. This
Budget is not aimed for the welfare of the people. Rather it is a Budget keeping in
mind the interests of corporate companies. Labourers and the farmers, who are
wealth creators of this country, are very much ignored in this Budget. At the same
time, there is paucity of time to speak here in detail. I cannot explain in detail within
one or two minutes of the allotted time. If we want to speak in detail there should be
many number of sittings for Parliament in one year. Parliament which had 135 sittings
per year now sits only for 55 days per year. This is like crucifying our democracy. You
should stop doing this but provide more opportunities to the Opposition parties. I wish
to state that only then we can explain in detail in this august House.
Tamil Nadu has no objection to the fact that Bihar has been given many
concessions in this Budget. But the question is why Tamil Nadu is targeted to be
ignored. Finance Minister should clarify why Tamil Nadu is being ignored.
Particularly the Opposition ruled States are completed ignored by this Union
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
10.02.2025 1106
Government. You target these States and destroy them. The victory which you got
in Delhi Elections is not permanent. This victory is of temporary nature. Division in
the Opposition has led to your victory. You might have engaged in some
irregularities. I wish to state that this victory of yours will not remain forever.
There is a mention of Tirukkural in Budget. But there is no justice or law in
this Budget. Similarly what will you do to increase employment in rural areas? You
should have strengthened the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment
Guarantee Scheme brought by the UPA Government. You should have increased
the wages given to the labourers under this Scheme. But you have reduced the
allocation from Rs. 89000 Crore to Rs 86000 Crore. You somehow want to scrap
this Scheme rather you are not concerned in providing regular employment to rural
people. During the last 10 years, after you came to power, how much benefits and
tax concessions were given to corporate companies? How did you waive off the
loans given to those corporate companies? How much the corporate tax has been
reduced? What was the loss to our government exchequer due to this? I want all
these facts should be released in the form of a ‘White Paper’ by this Union
Government. Only then it would be known for whom this Government is in power.
Similarly the Public Sectors Undertakings are mismanaged by you. You always
blame Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru. He left with us the PSUs as the wealth of the
people. These PSUs are the wealth of the general public. Where did you get this
power to disinvest all these PSUs? How can you act against the welfare of this
people? Have you got the permission from the people? Therefore you do not show
concern on anything. You see the insurance sector. Now 100 per cent FDI is
10.02.2025 1107
allowed in Insurance sector. Government has shed away all its responsibility from
insurance sector. This is injustice for the people and the country. Similarly the rivers
of South India. You have to link the rivers flowing in south India in order to make
south India fertile. This is a long pending demand. Only when you link all the rivers
of south India, entire south India would prosper. There won’t be any drought and
floods if the rivers are linked. This should be fulfilled.
What is the achievement of this Government of the Union? What is the value
of one rupee against one US Dollar or One Russian Ruble or currencies of other
countries? How much our rupee’s value has gone down? If the value has gone
down, who is responsible for that decline of Indian rupee? You are responsible for
that decline. I want to say one last thing. Our labourers are getting very less wages.
There is price rise on one side and inflation on other side affecting them. This price

rise is just like ... the wage of the poor and hardworking labour class of our society.
This Government is taking away their hard earned money. This Government do not
have any moral right to rule this country. This Budget is anti-people Budget;
corporate-friendly Budget and FDI-friendly Budget. I, therefore on behalf of
Communist Party of India, oppose this Budget. Thank you.

SHRI NAVASKANI K. (RAMANATHAPURAM): Madam Chairperson,
Vanakkam. I thank you for allowing me to take part in the discussion on Budget for
the year 2025-26. This Budget of the Union Government is ornamental wordplay

Expunged as ordered by the Chair.

English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
10.02.2025 1108
providing allocation on a partisan manner. Hon Finance Minister has not presented
here the Budget of BJP here. She has presented the Budget of this country. They
have given importance to the Election-bound States whereas negligence to the
Opposition ruled States of the country. That is why this Budget is partial. As there
is Assembly election due in Bihar, this Union Government has announced so many
welfare Schemes for Bihar. But this Union Budget has not taken care of the long
pending demands of Tamil Nadu with regard to its Highway projects, Railway
projects, funds for Metro rail projects in Madurai and Coimbatore, funds for Madurai
AIIMS, etc. You are large hearted for the States are ruled by you. You give very
less or do not give anything to the Opposition-ruled States of the country. Whether
is it justifiable? When you want to do politics, you will utilize Tamil Nadu. And if it is
for fund allocation, you will ignore the State of Tamil Nadu. Whether the people of
Tamil Nadu are visible to you only if elections are due? Whether Tamil Nadu is
visible to you only when you want to engage in politics? Please do show concern
towards people of Tamil Nadu not only in words but also in actions.
On the basis of National Educational policy you want to impose three
language policy on us. As we showed dissent to follow the National Educational
policy, you have threatened Tamil Nadu with dire consequences. You have taken
away Rs 2151 Crore meant for the students of Tamil Nadu and ensured that amount
is shared with other States, History will not forgive your actions which are disastrous
as regards the development of students in Tamil Nadu. By refusing to release
funds, you are trying to impose your National Educational Policy on us. Do you
think that Tamil Nadu will be afraid of your threat? You ask your forefathers. They
10.02.2025 1109
have witnessed the intensity of the Anti-Hindi agitations held by us. Whoever comes
as a threat to us, we will wipe them off from the History of Tamil Nadu. This Budget
is an attempt to show cat and hide an elephant behind. That is showing little and
hiding much. In the name of tax exemptions on one side, you have put the tax
burden and price rise on the other hand. Only 2 Crore people may get tax rebate
due to this announcement. Rather out of the total population of 140 Crore people,
remaining 138 Crore people will not be benefitted as you have not controlled price
rise. There is unprecedented unemployment problem. There is no alternative to
control this issue.
There is no announcement for bringing down the petrol and diesel prices.
Fertilizer subsidy, Gas Cylinder subsidy and other subsidies have been reduced.
You have increase this burden on the common man and just for name sake you
are announcing for tax relief. This is completely an eye wash. This is an attempt to
disappoint our people by ignoring their needs.
MNREGA Scheme is a scheme which is meant for uplifting the rural masses
of our country. This Scheme was brought by the Congress led Government in the
past. This BJP Government which is in favour of corporate companies is making all
attempts to slowly weaken this MGNREGA Scheme. The funds meant for this
Scheme are reduced gradually every year. This is an attempt which will affect the
livelihood of the people at the grass-root level. Rural people are showing objection
for merger of rural areas, and even the town panchayats with the municipalities as
they have a fear that MGNREGA would not be implemented in urban areas. This
MNREGA Scheme is so much helpful for the up-liftment of their lives. It is
10.02.2025 1110
unacceptable as you reduce the funds meant for MGNREGA year after year. I urge
that the funds for MNREGA should be increased besides increasing the daily
wages for labourers under this Scheme.
The attitude of this BJP Government has worsened during the last 10 years
of their rule. We can witness that the funds and Scholarships meant for Minority
students have seen a drastic reduction over the years. An amount of Rs 6360 Crore
was allocated in the previous Budget for the Pre-matric scholarship and Post-matric
scholarships meant for students of scheduled castes, scheduled tribes and
minorities. Out of which Rs 760 Crore remained unspent. Very less amount is
allocated for the overseas scholarships meant for Indian students pursuing courses
in foreign countries. Whereas the scholarships meant for research scholars have
been scrapped or stopped. This Government has stopped the scholarships issued
under Maulana Abul Kalam Azad Educational Foundation. This creates a doubt in
our mind whether this Government does not want the minority students to pursue
research. Why is this partiality? Why are you taking away the funds meant for the
education of minority students? For the pre-matric scholarships for students
belonging to minority communities, out of the allocated amount of Rs 326 Crore,
only Rs 90 Crore was spent. Moreover this year only Rs 195 Crore has been
allocated for this Scheme of Pre-matric scholarship. As regards Post-matric
scholarship, during the previous financial year, Rs 1045 Crore was allocated and
out of which a meagre amount of Rs 344 Crore was spent. Even this Post-matric
scholarship scheme has seen a reduction in allocation. The allocation is just Rs
413 Crore this year.
10.02.2025 1111
This Government has been reducing the fund allocation meant for minority
students time and again. This is aimed at attacking the minority students
educationally and economically. Now it wants to bring the Waqf Amendment Bill,
This Government has not brought any solution by finding ways to mitigate our
sufferings due to the previous Acts which were managing Waqf properties. There
were Schemes were used by us for managing and upgrading Waqf properties.
Shahari Waqf Sampatti Vikas Yojana gave a loan upto 2 Crore for improving the
Waqf properties. After I became the Chairman of Tamil Nadu Waqf Board, I started
verifying the fact as to how many States were receiving financial assistance under
this Scheme and the amount received by them. It was to my surprise that no loan
has been provided under this Scheme to any of the States during last three years.
These loans are to be issued after the approval of the State Waqf Council. So far,
during last 3 years, State Waqf Councils were not at all set up and which has
resulted in non-release of loans. This Government has not implemented any
Scheme for the welfare and development of minorities in the country, We have
been already facing difficulties in managing our Waqf properties and collecting rent
from the rented-out properties. Instead of evicting the encroachers on Waqf
properties, this Government now wants to acquire all the Waqf properties by means
of this new Waqf Amendment Bill. It is evident from its action that this government
wants to supress the minorities educationally and economically.
17.00 hrs
Basic healthcare facilities have not been provided to the common man. Only
a meagre amount of Rs 91,858 Crore was allocated to health care in the country.
10.02.2025 1112
The Central share has been gradually going down as regards the centrally
sponsored schemes of the government. The financial burden of the States is on the
rise. For name sake it is the Central Government’s Scheme and mostly the State
Government has to bear the cost. Prime Minister Housing Scheme is claimed to be
an achievement of the ruling BJP Government. Even our President’s Address has
mentioned about construction of 3 Crore houses in India under this Scheme. They
are just getting advertisement through this Scheme. During the last Budget, Rs
54,500 Crore was allocated for this Scheme. Out of which only Rs 32,400 Crore
was spent and approximately Rs 22,100 Crore remained unspent. For one Unit
under this Scheme Rs 1,11,000 will be borne by the Union Government and Rs
1,72,900 will be borne by the State Government. I must say only Rs 2,83,900 is
allocated for constructing a house under PM Awas Yojana. It is very difficult to
construct a house anywhere in India under this Scheme as the prices of Cement,
Steel, Brick etc. have gone high. Whether our Prime Minister and Finance Minister
are aware of this? In order to meet the price escalation, at least Rs 5 lakh should
be allocated for construction of one Unit under this Scheme. Hon. Chief Minister of
Tamil Nadu under the Kalaignar Dream Home Scheme provides a financial
assistance of Rs 5 lakh to construction of each house. Our Chief Minister of Tamil
Nadu is a role model for others to emulate. I therefore urge that Rs 5 lakh should
be allocated under PM Awas Yojana. Constructing 3 Crore houses by the Union
Government seems to be a mere announcement. Rather this Government should
reduce this number to 2 Crore house and increase the financial assistance for each
house. You should stop behaving in a partisan manner. You should treat all the
10.02.2025 1113
States of the country equally. Leaving aside election based politics, I urge that this
Government should really engage in constructive politics keeping in mind the
importance of our future generation. Thank you for this opportunity.

My esteemed colleagues, you will agree with me when I say that budgets
that are capable of pleasing everybody are very rare. It does not seem possible to
cut taxes, to reduce debt and deficit and, at the same time, increase investment in
growth promotion sectors and in the enhancement of infrastructure. This budget of
ours, the Budget of 2025-2026 of the Government of India, presented by Madam
Nirmala Sitharaman on 1st February, 2025, in this Lok Sabha, does all this and that
is why it is rare by nature.
10.02.2025 1120
Mr. Chairperson, Sir, please allow me to quote Martin Luther King Jr., the
civil rights leader. He had said:
“The ultimate measure of a man is not where he stands in the moment of
comfort. The ultimate measure of a man is where he stands during the time
of adversity, challenge and controversy.”
And I must tell you, that is to the credit of our hon. Prime Minister, Narendra
Modi ji and Madam Nirmala Sitharaman ji, that they handled the economy of India
with great deftness during difficult times, during COVID times, during the current
geopolitical conflicts in different parts of the world and in an atmosphere of
increased protectionism by different countries. I say with all pride and I say with all
conviction at my command that the efforts of these two leaders of our country have
ensured that we are the fastest growing among the major economies and we are
well on our way to becoming a five-trillion economy in the world.
Madam Nirmala Sitharaman, in her Budget on 1st February, said a few words.
I would request everyone to recall that. She said, “Our Government is committed to
keeping an ear to the ground and finger on the pulse while balancing nation building
efforts”. This budget is definitely a very pragmatic budget. It reflects the practical
approach of the Government and that is why it is extraordinary and it is rare.
My dear friends, I come from Odisha and, at the outset, again, I must thank
both the leaders, hon. Prime Minister, Narendra Modi ji and the Finance Minister,
Madam Nirmala Sitharaman, for the huge outlay in railway infrastructure. With a
double engine Government in Odisha, we have seen a lot of activities in the railway
sector in terms of more number of trains, in terms of more number of routes and in
10.02.2025 1121
terms of renovation of more number of railway stations.
Now, I also would like to mention here that there has been a boost in the
Tribal Affairs Ministry's budget by around Rs. 15,000 crore. It is about
Rs. 45,000 crore more and I am sure this increase will translate into a lot of tribal
welfare programs in States like Odisha.
Sir, to my mind, this particular Budget with an estimated expenditure of
around Rs.50,65,345 crore will be focusing on six major sectors. One, urban and
rural growth; two, investment; three, exports; four, middle-class relief; five,
innovation and technology; and six, infrastructure. बंधुगण, म (cid:167) आप सबको बताना चाह(cid:243)ंगी
िक यह बजट आंकड़(cid:332) और गणनाओ ंका बजट नह(cid:233) है, यह एक (cid:320)ि(cid:310)कोण है, यह एक िवज़न है । हम

अगर हमार े समाज, हमार े दशे को स(cid:320)ु ढ़ बनाना है, सश(cid:265) बनाना है तो म(cid:197)यम वग(cid:170) को स(cid:320)ु ढ़ और सश(cid:265)
बनाना होगा । That is why, Prime Minister Modi’s Government has put in around
Rs.1,00,000 crore into the pockets of the middle-class citizens and it is admirable.
I can tell you that this has been done by making the income up to Rs. 12,75,000 –
I include the standard deduction – completely tax free. This saving on account of
removal of this tax will definitely go towards more expenditure on education and
healthcare and, of course, there would be increased consumption spending.
My dear friends, I would request you to go back to 2005. In 2005, the income-
tax exemption limit was Rs.1,00,000. It took the then Prime Minister Dr. Manmohan
Singh’s Government seven years to actually double it to Rs.2,00,000 in 2012.
Please imagine about 10 years back, if your income had been Rs.12 lakh per
annum, you would have to pay Rs.2,60,000 as income-tax. Now I can say with all
10.02.2025 1122
conviction at my command that the middle-class of the country is heaving a sigh of
relief thanks to hon. Prime Minister and Shrimati Nirmala Sitharaman.
Some of the other aspects of the Budget are noteworthy. I just have to
mention those. All of us have heard Shrimati Nirmala Sitharaman, but I think at the
cost of being repetitive, I would like to list out some of those. First, the Jal Jeevan
Mission, under which about 12 crore tap connections have already been provided,
has been extended to 2028, and Rs.67,000 crore have been earmarked in the
Budget for the Jal Jeevan Mission. Second, the Budget announced PM Dhan-
Dhanya Krishi Yojana and this is extremely good for our annadata. I must tell you
that this particular scheme in partnership with the States and 100 districts will lead
to increased productivity, improve irrigation facility, and will make available short-
term and long-term credit very easily for our farmer friends.
More focus on fruits and vegetables, and pulses, are the other highlights of
the Budget which we must accept. The Government will set up an Urban Challenge
Fund for Rs.1,00,000 crore and that would be going a long way in the
implementation of proposals for city growth hubs. Who had thought of gig workers
in the country? Our Prime Minister did, and I am extremely proud of the fact that he
did. He talked of the gig workers of the online platforms. They would actually be
covered under the Pradhan Mantri Jan Aarogya Yojana and they will also be given
identity cards. A centre of excellence for artificial intelligence would be set up.
Rs.500 crore have been earmarked for artificial intelligence, and all of us wait and
watch. A lot of things could be done in this sector.
My dear friends, the Budget has also announced the creation of the National
10.02.2025 1123
Manufacturing Mission and the National Exports Mission. To my mind, these two
initiatives of the current Prime Minister Modi’s Government are extremely important
because they would be leading to manufacturing competitiveness, which is critical
to the growth of GDP in the country, and there would be global competitiveness of
our products which is good for all of us.
I am delighted to inform all of you that in these past 10 years, hon. Prime
Minister has been focusing on increasing export of goods from the country, and we
have almost touched USD 800 billion per annum worth of exports and the time is
not far, Mr. Chairman, Sir, when we would be touching USD 1 trillion worth of
exports in the country. Carefully planned focus on a couple of sectors, leather and
toy sectors, agriculture and marine sectors, textile and handicraft sectors and
MSME will lead to more jobs, more exports and industrial growth.
Sir, we all know that the total Budget of India at this moment, Rs. 50,65,345
crore in terms of percentage of the GDP is less than 20 per cent. I admit it, but I
say with all conviction at my command that a budget of a country is not just some
figures on a page. The budget of a country reflects the intention of the Government
and the kind of direction which the Government wants the country to take. So, this
Budget is definitely an expression of the great intention of Prime Minister Modi's
idea of having a Viksit Bharat. It is not a mean achievement. My dear friends,
cutting across party lines, all my friends, my colleagues in the Lok Sabha, we need
to appreciate that during the COVID-19 times, the fiscal deficit of the country was
9.2 per cent of the GDP, and it has been brought down to an estimated 4.4 per cent
of the GDP in this Financial Year 2025-26 Budget. So, a growth of 7 per cent and
10.02.2025 1124
above is not a mean achievement.
Sir, I talked of all the problems that are going on in this world, geopolitical
conflicts that I talked of; I talked of the protectionism approach of many of the
countries in which we have to perform, I talked of the COVID-19 issues. A growth
of 7 per cent and above, I must say, has led to two things which we must appreciate
and acknowledge whether we like it or not. One, we have become the fastest
growing economy among the major economies. Number two, of course, this is an
example of fiscal prudence. We have been able to handle our financial aspect
extremely well.
So, with these words, I lend my full support to the Budget which was
presented by Madam Nirmala Sitharaman on 1st February 2025.
Thank you very much. Jai Jagannath!

SHRI DURAI VAIKO (TIRUCHIRAPPALLI): Thank you, Sir for providing me an
opportunity to speak on the Union Budget. Federalism is an integral part of our
republic that ensures diversity, peace, stability, and mutual accommodation in a
multi-cultural and diversified country like ours. But unfortunately, this Union Budget
has abandoned the key aspect of federalism.
We do not have any objection with regard to Bihar receiving Rs.11,500 crore
for flood control measures. At the same time, why do States like Tamil Nadu and
Kerala that are heavily hit by natural disasters year by year not get the necessary
financial assistance from the Union Government? The Tamil Nadu Government
repeatedly pleaded for Rs.37,906 crore as financial assistance from the Union
Government when cyclone Michaung devasted our northern districts. And an
unprecedented heavy rainfall caused havoc in the southern districts in the year
2023. Despite the Supreme Court’s intervention, we got a paltry Rs. 276 crore, not
 Expunged as ordered by the Chair.
10.02.2025 1127
even one per cent of the financial assistance. When cyclone Fengal devastated our
northern districts again, last year, our Chief Minister, Mr. M.K. Stalin, repeatedly
requested for Rs. 6,675 crore as financial assistance under the National Disaster
Response Fund. But unfortunately, we have not received a penny so far. We are
yet to receive Rs. 2,152 crore. The pending funds under the Samagra Shiksha
Abhiyan scheme has negatively impacted the Tamil Nadu School Education
Department, which is not able to pay for the teachers’ salary and maintenance of
the Government’s schools, nor reimburse the expenses under the Right to
Education Act.
We are yet to receive Rs. 26,000 crore with respect to the Union
Government’s share of the Chennai Metro Rail Phase-II. Then, Rs. 1,635 crore for
the MGNREGA wages in Tamil Nadu are pending for the past two months. There
have been no special announcement of any project for Tamil Nadu. There has also
not been any mention of metro rail projects for cities like Madurai, Coimbatore and
Trichy.
Sir, our hon. Prime Minister Modi ji, the other day, in a reply to the Motion of
Thanks quoted Ambedkar ji’s vision of linking all the rivers of this country. But sadly,
this Government has been sitting on the Cauvery-Vaigai-Gundar linking project for
a very long time. There has been no mention in this Budget regarding that particular
river linking project which will greatly benefit our State of Tamil Nadu.
Sir, my State of Tamil Nadu has been repeatedly neglected in the Union
Budget. Tamil Nadu is the second largest State, and economically, it is contributing
nearly 8.8 per cent to the nation’s GDP, and it is the third-largest exporting State
10.02.2025 1128
contributing ten per cent to the country’s total exports. In spite of all these things,
Tamil Nadu’s share in the total divisible pool of Central taxes, has been reduced to
just four per cent. Tamil Nadu which only finds a place in the Indian map, does not
find a place in the Union Budget nor in the hearts of the Finance Minister or the
Prime Minister.
Sir, ‘Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas’, that is, ‘collective efforts and inclusive
growth’ is a wonderful slogan coined by our Prime Minister Modi ji. I would like to
tell our Prime Minister Modi ji that ‘Sabka Saath’, that is, ‘collective efforts’ is already
there. The entire nation, the citizens of this country, and even the Opposition Parties
are there with you in taking this country forward. But ‘Sabka Vikas’, that is, ‘inclusive
growth’, sadly, is not happening.
Sir, this Union Budget which is supposed to be drafted for the Union of
States, that is, India, has been drafted just for the BJP-ruled States. ‘Sabka Saath,
Sabka Vikas’ should be in letter and spirit for us to have a developed India by 2047.
HON. CHAIRPERSON: Please conclude.
SHRI DURAI VAIKO: Sir, I will just conclude in one minute.
Our Finance Minister has called this Budget, ‘By the people, for the people,
and of the people’. After going through the Budget, I wondered what she meant by
‘people’. I am sure it is not a Budget for the poor or oppressed classes. It is not a
Budget for the farmers. It is not a Budget for the unemployed. But I am sure that
this is a Budget for the BJP Karyakartas. It is a Budget for the BJP’s allies. And it
is a Budget customised for the upcoming Bihar Assembly elections.
I, once again, reiterate ‘Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas’, that is, ‘collective efforts
10.02.2025 1129
and inclusive growth’ should be in letter and spirit for us to have a developed India
by the year 2047.
Thank you, Sir.

DR. T. SUMATHY ALIAS THAMIZHACHI THANGAPANDIAN (CHENNAI
SOUTH): Hon. Chairman, Vanakkam. Hon. Finance Minister in the beginning Para
of her Budget Speech quoted the lines of famous Telugu Poet Gurajada Apparao,
“A country is not just its soil; a country is its people”. I want to say to Hon. Finance
Minister that ‘Dravidam’ is not mere soil or place. Dravidian race is as special as it
has the distinction of being called as the people with less superstitions around the
world by the eminent linguist Caldwell. As a member of this Dravidian race, I am
deeply pained to state that the people of Tamil Nadu who are the majority of this
race, are ignored in this Union Budget. I express my views in this august House in
Tamil which is claimed by Mahakavi Bharathi as the sweetest language among all
the languages. He says, “Among all the languages that I have known, there is none
as sweet as Tamil”. This is the eighth consecutive Budget presented by the Finance
Minister Smt. Nirmala Sitaraman. This Budget is beyond reach for the poor,
farmers, transgender people, oppressed and suppressed people, and minorities
and most particularly for the people of Tamil Nadu. “Water, water everywhere, not
a drop to drink” is a famous line of Coleridge. There is water everywhere. But there
is not even a single drop to drink. Mahakavi Bharathi says, “We will make weapons
and paper too. We will start factories as well as schools. We will not rest or drop
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
10.02.2025 1130
our heads.” As per the lines of Mahakavi Bharathi, our hon. Chief Minister of Tamil
Nadu Thalapathi Thiru M. K. Stalin is providing good governance in Tamil Nadu in
an exemplary manner. Hon. Chief Minister has made several demands to the Union
Government. As it is said “everywhere water and not a drop to drink”, you have
extended with a large heart all the favours only to the States ruled by BJP and your
allies. You have rather shown empty hands to Tamil Nadu. That is why we call this
a partisan Budget and a Budget which is ignoring us.
Hon. Chairman, just think about how Budget is presented throughout the
world? Canada and Germany provide a part of their spending for social welfare,
social security, child welfare and skill development schemes. But this Union
Government has been continuously reducing the allocation meant for social welfare
schemes in our country. You can take for example Schemes like MGNREGA and
PMKISAN. But this Government is providing lots of subsides to the corporate
companies. It is transferring the tax amount collected from the common people to
the corporate giants. I will hence call this as the Budget transferring justice to
injustice. Of all the countries to improve public governance and finance, if you see,
innovative Budget is presented by them. Brazil gives the voting right to its citizens
to select municipality expenses. This ensures the public participation in the making
of their Budget. I am here reminded of Hon Muthamizh Arignar Dr. Kalaignar, who
before presenting every Budget, had extensive consultations with small and micro
traders, street vendors and common people. Our present Chief Minister Thalapathi
Thiru M.K. Stalin has been providing an exclusive Budget for farmers of the State
of Tamil Nadu. I am gratefully remembering him at this point of time. You look at
10.02.2025 1131
New Zealand. It is people’s welfare budget. If you look at Norway, it is an
environment-oriented Budget. The Netherlands presents an Outcome-based
Budget. After fixing your responsibility, funds will be allocated as per your
performance. It is the system of getting funds released as a right. A senior Union
Minister recently said that Tami Nadu’s demand for more funds as per its increased
tax contribution is ‘petty thinking’. If it is ‘petty thinking’, an amount of Rs 2152
Crore given to Tamil Nadu under PMSHRI Scheme has been diverted to Bihar and
Uttar Pradesh. Is it not “petty thinking” on your part? Are we not supposed to ask
this amount back from them? Just ponder over this. You give funds to Bihar. We
do not have any objection. When there were heavy floods in Bihar, the then Chief
Minister Dr. Kalaignar sent Rs. 10 Crore as flood relief fund. When he passed away,
the State of Bihar was saddened and observed grief. There is no second thought
on this. You have given Rs 60000 Crore to Bihar during last Budget and now you
have provided Makhana Board, IIT in Patna and Kosi Canal Project to Bihar in this
current Budget. If that is so, can’t we expect our farmers to develop in their lives?
Hon Chairman Sir, there is some time left for me to speak in the allocated
time. Maharashtra has been provided Rs. 39,000 crore. But there is no major
announcement regarding money allocated to the Schemes of Tamil Nadu. Our hon.
Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu Thalapathi Thiru M. K. Stalin has announced to the
world the greatness of Tamil and the antiquity of iron through fresh archaeological
findings. But during the last Budget, Tamil language was only provided Rs. 22 crore.
During the current Budget, more than Rs. 1000 crore has been allocated to
promotion of Hindi language whereas Tamil language has been provided only a
10.02.2025 1132
meagre amount of Rs. 84 crore. Hon. Chairman Sir, you look at their economic
survey. It says that GSDP of Tamil Nadu is 56 per cent which is more than the
national average. It also says that Tamil Nadu contributes 9 percent to the national
GDP. Sir, please provide two more minutes. Tamil Nadu ranks in top three places
as regards Education, medicine, and all other sectors. If you are ignoring us, how
can it be justifiable? Where is justice? This Government has given a quote of
Spartan soldier in the preface of the Economic Survey. It says, “The more you
sweat for peace, the less you bleed in war”. I can say this with some changes to
you. “The more you sweat in inclusive politics, the less you bleed in economic
progress”. You have kept the ‘Sengol” - the sceptre, in this august House. The
place does not make the man; neither sceptre; the King’s greatness should be
within. The sceptre does not make a King rather his virtues should come from
within. You should understand the values of democracy and the philosophy of
federalism. Please give up religious polarisation and adopt secular politics. This
will lead to prosperity and happiness of people. Thank you.
SHRI TANGELLA UDAY SRINIVAS (KAKINADA): Sir, I stand before this august
House today with deep gratitude to the people of Kakinada for electing me as their
representative. I also extend my heartfelt thanks to my party leader Shri Pawan
Kalyan Garu for his unwavering trust and support and to our Chief Minister, Shri N.
Chandrababu Naidu Garu for his visionary leadership. As this is my first speech in
Parliament, it is both an honour and a responsibility which I carry with utmost
sincerity and commitment.
Sir, over the last few days, I have listened carefully to my esteemed
10.02.2025 1133
colleagues in this House. Some of their words have inspired hope and ambition,
while others have raised concerns as to whether the Government is doing enough
for the common men and the middle-class people. However, as I delve deeper into
the Budget, the policies and the continuous push for progress, I find it difficult to
understand where this criticism stems from.
I am new to this field. But I have studied the Government initiatives. One
thing has become clear. The NDA government is not only aware of the challenges
faced by the middle-class people, but is also actively working to address them.
Take for instance, the recent announcement by our hon. Finance Minister, Shrimati
Nirmala Sitharaman ji regarding the zero-tax liability for income up to Rs.12 lakh.
When I first heard about it, like millions of Indians, I was elated.
The impact of this decision is massive. Out of 7.5 crore taxpayers who filed
returns in the financial year 2023-2024, nearly 6.5 crore people earned below Rs.12
lakh per annum. This means that over 85 per cent of the taxpayers will now be free
from income tax liability. Why is this significant? It is because India’s economy is a
consumption-driven economy where private consumption accounts nearly for 60
per cent of our GDP. More disposable income means higher spending, stronger
demand and boost for business, ultimately propelling us towards the vision of a 5
trillion dollar economy.
Let us look at the bigger picture. India’s income tax multiplier is around 1.01.
This means that every single rupee saved in taxes does not just stay invalid. It
multiplies, ultimately adding to the GDP. This is not just about the tax relief. It is
about empowering individuals, strengthening business and fuelling the economic
10.02.2025 1134
engine that drives India forward. Under the NDA government, GDP is not just Gross
Domestic Product but the growth of the economy, development of the citizens and
progress of the nation. This vision is backed by the action.
In just 10 years, India’s capital expenditure has increased from Rs.2 lakh
crore to Rs.11 lakh crore, a five-fold increase, laying a foundation for long-term
economic strength. But economic growth is not just about large-scale infrastructure.
It is also about empowering business and workers at every level. The Union Budget
of 2025 has rightfully recognised micro, small, medium enterprises, MSMEs, as a
critical engine for economic growth, providing them with a renewed impetus to
create reforms and increased credit support. At the same time, the evolving gig
economy is receiving the attention it deserves. Initiatives such as registration
through e-Shram portal and health care benefits under PM Jan Arogya Yojana will
ensure that gig and platform workers have better access to social security,
reinforcing the Government’s vision of inclusive economic development.
Sir, as a proud representative of Kakinada, I stand for our fishermen who
battle both against the sea and financial insecurity. The NDA Government eased
the burden by extending the Kisan Credit Card, benefiting over 4.5 lakh fishermen.
This Budget will increase the credit limit further. It helps to invest in better boats,
modern nets, and coal storage. This is not just financial support. It is a commitment
to the resilience and prosperity.
As we discuss the economic growth, we must not forget that nearly 65 per
cent of the Indian population resides in rural areas, contributing almost 50 per cent
of the GDP. Mahatma Gandhi once said, “The soul of India lives in its village.” Yet,
10.02.2025 1135
this very soul was deprived of its lifeblood, that is, clean drinking water by the
previous State Government of Andhra Pradesh. Jal Jeevan Mission was launched
as a flagship initiative to provide safe drinking water to every rural household.
Despite the Centre allocating over Rs.16,000 crore, the previous State Government
of Andhra Pradesh managed to draw only Rs.2,000 crore, leaving lakhs of
households without access to proper drinking water facilities.
Now, under the decisive leadership of Shri Pawan Kalyan Garu, Deputy Chief
Minister, hon. Minister of Rural Development and Panchayati Raj, our NDA
Government is committed to rectifying its past failures. Understanding the urgency,
Shri Pawan Kalyan Garu personally met hon. Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi
and the Union Minister of Jal Sakthi to secure an extension of JJM and additional
funds. Additionally, to accelerate the rural development, Shri Pawan Kalyan Garu
launched the landmark Palle Panduga programmme, setting a Guinness World
Record for the largest number of Gram Sabhas held in a single day. About 13,326
village meetings were held. As a part of this remarkable initiative, 30,000
development projects were launched across Andhra Pradesh. About Rs.4,500
crore were sanctioned for rural roads, water conservation, and MGNREGS
projects.
This is not just a Government initiative but a grass-root revolution. Another
testament to Shri Pawan Kalyan Garu’s dedication to Andhra Pradesh was seen at
the Republic Day celebration. As an active advocate for Etikoppaka toys, his effort
brought them into national spotlight, leading to their showcase in the Andhra
Pradesh Republic Day tableau. The result is that, after 30 years, Andhra Pradesh's
10.02.2025 1136
tableau won the best tableau at the Republic Day Parade in Delhi, honouring our
artisans and cultural heritage. This is the new Andhra Pradesh under the NDA–one
that is recognized, respected, and rapidly progressing.
Now, let me highlight the vision of self-reliance, which is not only rooted in
cultural pride, but also deeply embedded in our economic and strategic policies.
This vision is mirrored in the Union Budget 2025. Take, for example, the cotton
sector. India accounts for approximately 40 per cent of the world's cotton cultivation
area, with 126.80 lakh hectares dedicated to cotton farming. A decade ago, this
stood at around 118 lakh hectares, reflecting steady expansion. However, despite
this vast acreage, India's cotton productivity remains significantly lower than
countries like China, Australia, Brazil, and the US.
To address this, the Union Budget has introduced the Cotton Technology
Mission, a five-year initiative aimed at enhancing productivity through scientific, and
technological innovations. This mission will focus on promoting extra-long staple
(ELS) cotton, reducing our dependency on imports, and ensuring sustainability.
This comprehensive approach will not only elevate the cotton sector but also
strengthen India's position as a global textile leader.
The Budget's commitment to self-reliance is also evident in the maritime
sector. India's dependency on foreign shipping has led to significant financial
outflows, with Indian companies paying $85 billion in freight costs in 2019-20, $75
billion of which went to foreign vessels. To curb this, the budget outlines the
establishment of shipbuilding clusters to strengthen the domestic shipbuilding
industry.
10.02.2025 1137
Andhra Pradesh, with its 974 kilometres coastline and strategic ports, is well-
positioned to drive India's shipbuilding ambitions. The State's robust maritime
ecosystem, coupled with the Andhra Pradesh Maritime Policy 2024-29, will play a
critical role in reducing foreign dependency.
Among its port cities, Kakinada stands out as a prime candidate to lead this
transformation. With a natural deep-water port, proximity to major trade routes, and
existing industrial infrastructure, Kakinada is well on its way to becoming the
shipbuilding hub of Andhra Pradesh.
In conclusion, Sir, the vision for India is clear. From toy to tech, from farm to
future, and from infrastructure to innovation, we are building a nation that is self-
reliant, inclusive, and future-ready.
Thank you.
