SHRI G. KUMAR NAIK (RAICHUR): (Hon. Chairperson, Sir, the Union Budget
presented by the hon. Finance Minister Smt. Nirmala Sitharaman ji is not a mirror
that reflects the reality. It is not even a lamp that guides us about the future. It is

merely a bundle of … . It is just a statement of half deliveries. It is just slogans of
ambitions and it is merely a small booklet that is far away from the real problems
and agonies of the common people of the country. Today the development of Indian
economy is moving at a very slow pace. Inflation rate is very high. As far as
inequality is concerned a new chapter has been created. This is a matter of great
concern for all of us. Unemployment has touched its peak. In totality, life of common
people has become miserable.*
Sir, this Budget is neither a mirror that reflects our prevailing reality, nor a
lamp that illuminates the path forward. Instead, it is a fine piece of creative fiction,
bold in its claims, ambitious in its slogans, and utterly disconnected from the lived
experiences of the people.
 ….. English translation of this part of speech originally delivered in Kannada.
 Not recorded.
10.02.2025 1142
The GDP growth has plummeted from 8.2 per cent to 6.4 per cent. Year-on-
year inflation in the Consumer Food Price Index has surged to 8.39 per cent,
squeezing the household budgets. Inequality is at an all-time high, with the top one
per cent of the population controlling a staggering 40 per cent of the country's
wealth. Unemployment is at a dangerous level impacting the social harmony. This
is not an isolated issue, but an all-India phenomenon.
Shri Rahul Gandhi walked across the country, listened to the struggles of the
poor and turned their pain into action, bringing the guarantee schemes that ensure
a universal basic income for those who need it most. But what does this
Government do? It announces income tax benefits to those earning 12 times the
per capita income of this country. Also, the write-off of loans of rich defaulters
continues. If helping the poor is a rebdi, then what do you call this?
Let us now turn our attention to the gross injustice faced by Karnataka State.
The 15th Finance Commission drastically reduced Karnataka’s share of Central
taxes by 23 per cent from 4.7 per cent to 3.6 per cent, the steepest decline among
the States. Given that the Finance Commission is a Constitutional body, the
Finance Ministers' hands will be soon tied once again by the 16th Finance
Commission Report. The Report must not be reduced to a mere theoretical
exercise. It must reflect the ground realities. We must seek a framework that
upholds balanced development, cooperative federalism, social harmony and a
sense of financial justice. States must receive their rightful share. It is not a fiction
of imagination.
With regard to railways, the Budget has misplaced priorities in my opinion.
10.02.2025 1143
While we need a robust railway network across the country, the Government is
focusing disproportionately on the showpiece trains such as Vande Bharat and
Namo Bharat. These trains serve already well-connected regions, ignoring the
needs of the underserved areas.
Sir, Karnataka has one of the lowest railway densities in the country. You
must be astonished to hear this. For every 100 square kilometres, Karnataka has
only 2.62 kilometres of railway tracks, whereas Uttar Pradesh has more than twice
and West Bengal has more than four times this length. When I met the Railway
Minister, he acknowledged this disparity with concern. It is heartening to see that
the issue is recognized at the highest levels, but recognition alone will not lay down
railway tracks. I sincerely hope the Government take immediate steps to introduce
much-needed railway projects, particularly Alamatti-Yadgiri railway line.
As the Finance Minister rightly stated, agriculture is the first engine of growth.
Rashtrakavi Kuvempu compares the life of a farmer with that of a yogi:

Unknown to others, the plough holding ascetic
Provides food to the world
Undesiring reputation, undesiring extreme comfort
He works without expecting honour.*
In 2016, the Prime Minister promised to double the farmers’ income by 2022.
Yet today, forget doubling of income, more than half the country’s farmers are in
deep debt. Karnataka’s farmers produce some of the finest chillies in the country.
….. English translation of this part of speech originally delivered in Kannada.
10.02.2025 1144
Last season we had an excellent yield, but due to market failures, tonnes of chillies
are now lying unsold in cold storage. What good of a bumper harvest if the farmers
cannot get a fair price for their produce? Just as there is crop insurance for losses
due to natural calamities, there must also be safeguards against market failures.
That being said, I welcome the announcement of the Mission for Cotton Productivity
in this Budget. However, while designing the programme, I urge the Government
to consult the farmers extensively.
While on the topic, I would like to acknowledge the Finance Minister for
allocating Rs. 50 lakh for FPOs from her MPLAD funds, to each of the Kalyana
Karnataka districts. This reflects her concern for our region. I hope she extends the
same generosity to all the pressing issues of our region.
As the Finance Minister rightly stated, the MSMEs are the second engine of
growth. But here, what has not been said seems more important. Between 2016
and 2021, 1.3 crore workers vanished from the informal sector, 24 lakh enterprises
shut down, and manufacturing employment fell by 81 lakh jobs. This Budget talks
about increasing loan limits under various schemes, but the reality is that it is
unlikely to be of any help because the average MSME loan size in backward
regions, like mine, under the MUDRA scheme is just Rs. 50,000. The banks are not
lending to MSMEs in these regions but are very busy writing off massive loans for
big business friends of this Government. This is the part of the story that is unsaid.
Speaking of economic vulnerabilities, Ayushman Bharat, despite its flaws,
has helped the poor access tertiary healthcare. Tertiary healthcare infrastructure is
crucial for ensuring equitable access to specialized medical services. Yet,
10.02.2025 1145
Karnataka is the only major State, I repeat, Karnataka is the only major State that
still lacks an AIIMS. AIIMS Horata Samiti Raichur is on the streets, in blistering sun
and rain agitating for AIIMS in Raichur for over thousand days now. As the Raichur
MP, my demand is, we want AIIMS for Karnataka. Karnataka wants AIIMS in
Raichur and this demand must be met forthwith.
Education is the foundation of any developed nation. The UPA Government
under the leadership of our former Prime Minister, Dr. Manmohan Singh, and
Shrimati Sonia Gandhi made education a right, recognizing its transformational
capacity. Today, instead of building on this legacy, this Government is asking
students, who are poor, to take loans to study, shifting the burden of financing
education onto young people. Education has been reduced to a set of schemes –
PM SHRI, PM Vidyalaxmi, etc. Why is the Government negotiating with private
institutions only for healthcare? Why not do the same for education and bring in
‘Vidhyavan Bharat’ akin to Ayushman Bharat to ensure universal access? You are
doing so as if you are not willing to invest on infrastructure and teachers’ salaries.
Education must be brought back on the right path. The SCs and STs make up 25
per cent of India’s population. … (Interruptions)

सराहनीय है ।

SHRI RAJABHAU PARAG PRAKASH WAJE (NASHIK): Hon’ble Chairman Sir,
I would like to thank you for giving this opportunity to speak on the Union Budget.
It is mentioned in the budget that agriculture is a growth engine of our economy.
But we have to ensure that the farmers are getting the benefits of welfare schemes.
Today, we are self-reliant in production of good grains just because of their sweat
and blood. But due to increasing production cost, climate changes and no guaranty
of reasonable prices, the farmers have to face problems. There are certain
declarations made by the Government in this budget, but we have to take concrete
steps for their smooth and speedy implementation. I demand to include Nashik
District under PM Food grains scheme. The Union Government is working towards
achieving self-reliance in pulse production, but MSP should be fixed for it. It should
also be purchased on the basis of MSP and a system should be developed to
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
10.02.2025 1150
ensure it.
Prices of fertilizer, seeds and insecticides should be controlled by
Government or there should be a special fund to compensate it. Finance Minister
has reduced income tax burden and I am really very thankful for it, but GST on
pesticides should also be reduced.
Sir, health and medical treatment facilities should also be made available in
rural areas. These facilities are only on paper and it is not being implemented
properly. Hence, their proper implementation is needed.
As of now, Kumbh Mela is organized at Prayagraj, but after 2 years, it will be
organized at Trimbakeshwar, Nashik in 2027. It would be a world class religious
celebration which will boost travel and tourism. So, we have to provide basic
amenities for it. But the Central Government has not provided any fund for it, so
that should be done. To clean Godawari river, we have to operate a plant there and
it should be done right now for timely completion. Kavnai, Taket, Trimbakeshwar
and Nashik are the places where it is to be organized. So, these places should be
ready with all kind of infrastructure and facilities.
Nashik is a well-known district for the production of Onions and grapes. But,
the farmers there have to suffer losses due to decline in prices. So, market driven
policy should be implemented. Export duty on onions should be minimized or
removed completely. Government is charging 20% export duty on onions currently,
which needs to be removed immediately.
New irrigation projects should be started by the Government. PM Irrigation
scheme should also be implemented effectively. More funds should be sanctioned
10.02.2025 1151
for farm lakes and irrigation facilities. Benefits should be provided to common
farmers with the extended limits of Rs. 5 lacs under Kisan Credit Card.
A medical tourism centre should be developed in Nashik under Heal in India
scheme which would be beneficial during Kumbha Mela. A new medical hospital
should be constructed to provide medical facilities during Kumbh as the facilities
there are not up to the mark.
Sir, I would like to reiterate that farmers are the backbone of our economy
and we cannot stand without them. We cannot develop without their development.
Hence, all the declarations made in this budget, should be implemented properly.
Government should come towards to ensure modern agriculture system in India. It
should be price and market driven for the welfare of farmers. We must work towards
ensuring a prosperous life for our farmers.
Lastly, more funds should be released for Nashik Kumbh Mela and Nashik-
Pune Railway line project.
With these words, I conclude.
Jai Hind, Jai Maharashtra.

SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN (KOLLAM): Thank you very much Mr. Chairman
Sir.
Sir, I rise to oppose the Budget proposals for the financial year 2025-26 as
the Budget 2025-26 is not addressing the genuine concerns and basic
requirements of the common people of the country.
Sir, the basic challenge the Indian economy is facing is the problem of
demand. Instead of addressing the problem of demand in the economy due to the
lack of purchasing power in the hands of the people because of mass
unemployment and shrinking wages, the Finance Minister has through this Budget
sought to stimulate the economy by giving concessions to the rich and even by
cutting the expenditure.
Sir, instead of mobilising resources by taxing the rich and the big corporate
houses and expanding public investment which would help generate employment
and ensure a minimum wage to the people, the Government has chosen the
opposite direction.
10.02.2025 1164
Sir, the Union Budget 2025-26 absolutely neglected the issue of
unemployment and expenditure on food subsidies, agriculture and allied activities,
education, rural development, social welfare and urban development are stagnant
or lower than that of the previous financial year if you factor in inflation also.
Sir, I can very well substantiate this observation or this assessment as far as
this Budget is concerned with the support of statistics.
Sir, I do appreciate that the Government is able to collect the tax as
envisaged in 2024-25 Budget because Rs. 25 lakh crore of tax collection is
envisaged in the Budget proposal. That has been collected for which I appreciate
the Government and also. I appreciate the fact that for up to Rs. 12 lakh of annual
income, it is zero income tax. This is a welcome step.
Other than these two steps, Sir, if you critically examine the Budget
proposals, it is nothing but full of promises and making slogans of having a Viksit
Bharat by 2047. Nothing is being proposed. It is not a growth-oriented Budget. It is
absolutely giving profits, concessions, exemptions and benefits to the corporates
and nothing for the common people at large.
Sir, I will substantiate this matter. Further, one of the major allegations, not
allegation, it is an assessment of the entire Opposition in the country that the Indian
economy is slowing down. That is proved as correct and substantiated and ratified
in the Budget proposals. If you examine it, it is very clear the allegation or the
observation or the assessment of the Opposition, the Indian economy is slowing
down is proved to be correct if you examine the Budget proposals.
Sir, the Government is always claiming that it is the fastest growing economy
10.02.2025 1165
in the world, the third largest economy in the world; it will become a five trillion dollar
economy soon and we will achieve the goal of Viksit Bharat by 2047. Big
propaganda and big PR management are going on. I would like to ask a specific
question to the hon. Finance Minister as to whether the Budget proposal of 2025-
2026 is sufficient enough to achieve the goal of Viksit Bharat by 2047.
Even the Chief Economic Advisor to the Government specifically stated in
The Economic Survey that if you want to achieve the goal of Viksit Bharat by 2047,
eight per cent GDP growth is required annually. Is it correct? But you are envisaging
only 6.8 per cent GDP growth rate in this year. If that be the case, how are we
going to achieve the so-called goal of Viksit Bharat or developed economy by 2047?
This the first question which I would like to ask the Government.
Sir, considering the performance of the Government, the major chunk of the
current year’s Budget was to address the unemployment problem. So many
programmes were announced like PLI scheme, new employment generation
scheme, Skill India Programme etc., It was the announcement made by the hon.
Finance Minister in the last Budget that it will create thousands and thousands of
employment.
Sir, the Budget Estimate was Rs.26,018 crore and so far, the actual Budget
expenditure is just Rs.15,286 crore. How are we going to address the
unemployment issue? DBT scheme for first-time job seekers, Credit Guarantee
Scheme for the MSMEs – all these schemes are in the formulation stage only.
Nothing has taken off. For addressing the unemployment problem, nothing has
been done because the proposals are not realistic.
10.02.2025 1166
The second point which I would like to make is that the Budget is totally anti-
federal. As various hon. Members have cited, devolution of tax revenue to the
States is drastically declining through the Budget proposals. If you kindly examine
the share of cesses and surcharges in the Central Government's Gross Tax
Revenue, in 2014-15, the State’s share in the divisible pool was 32 per cent. But
the States have got only 27 per cent. The share of surcharges in the Central
Government’s Gross Tax Revenue in 2025-26 is 13 per cent.
When this government came to power in 2014-15, it was 9 per cent of GTR.
If you kindly see, the State’s share in 2025-26, it is estimated at only 33 per cent.
The divisible pool is arrived after excluding the surcharges. As Shrimati Supriya
Sule has already stated, 37 items are there in the form of tariffs and customs. The
customs tariff has been drastically reduced and the Agricultural Infrastructure and
Development Cess has been incorporated.
I will cite one or two examples. For marble and granite, the existing customs
tariff was 40 per cent. It has been reduced to 20 per cent and 20 per cent
Agricultural Infrastructure and Development Cess has been added. In the case of
solar module, the customs tariff has been reduced to 40 per cent and 20 per cent
AIDC has been added. In the case of footwear, the customs tariff was 35 per cent.
It has been reduced to 20 per cent and 18.5 per cent AIDC has been added.
The Finance Commission is awarding 41 per cent of divisible pool but
unfortunately, you are getting only 33 per cent because surcharge and cess is
increasing day by day. I would like to know from the two political parties, Janata Dal
(United) and the Telugu Desam party which are supporting the BJP led
10.02.2025 1167
Government at the Centre as to whether they are supporting this thing because the
State’s share is being drastically taken away by means of the proposals of the
Budget. It is against the Finance Commission’s award; it is against the fiscal
federalism of the country which is being pursued since long.
Sir, the argument of the Government is that we are having so many Centrally-
Sponsored Schemes by which the share of the State Government has been
increased. We can also critically examine this. In the Budget of 2024-25, the
allocation for Centrally-Sponsored Schemes was Rs.5.05 lakh crore. But as per the
Revised Estimate, it is just Rs.4.15 lakh crore, 18 per cent lower than that of the
Budget Estimate. It means that about Rs.90,000 crore are not being spent as far
as the CSS is concerned. Similarly, the allocation to the various ministries also has
not been utilised.
As far as the shift in the tax collection is concerned, in 2013-14, before this
Government came to power, the corporate tax was just 3.5 per cent of the GDP,
and the income tax was just 2.1 per cent of the GDP. Now after 10 years of BJP
Government, the income tax collection has come to Rs. 14.4 lakh crore, that is,
4.03 per cent of the GDP. The direct tax collection, and the corporate tax collection
have come down 3.03 per cent of the GDP, that is, Rs.10.8 lakh crore. That means,
it is a corporate-centric Budget which is being pronounced for all the 10 years. That
is the reason why we are saying that it is anti-common people.
Sir, another very important point is regarding fiscal consolidation. I would
like to seek a clarification from the hon. Finance Minister. When the FRBM Act
2003 was enacted, there was a strict stipulation that after every three years,
10.02.2025 1168
continued three years, there would be a progressive deduction of the fiscal deficit,
revenue deficit, and general debt. It is in respect of fiscal deficit. But, now, in para
109 of the Budget Speech of the hon. Finance Minister, it is being stated: “We will
take our endeavour of the Government to bring down the outstanding liabilities to
around 50 per cent of the GDP by 2031.” But there is no proposal in the Finance
Bill 2025 to amend the FRBM Act. I would like to know from the hon. Finance
Minister without amending the FRBM Act how the Government is going to
implement this new programme.
There is another Constitutional question regarding the new schemes. Sir, in
the Budget of 2024-25, vide Entry 27, Demand Number 30, new schemes were
incorporated for the Department of Economic Affairs, and Rs. 62,593 crore were
allocated. Out of this, only Rs. 9,000 crore had been spent. I would like to know
about these new schemes because the Parliament is not aware.
HON. CHAIRPERSON: Hon. Member, please conclude now.
… (Interruptions)
SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN: Sir, I will conclude.… (Interruptions)
How will this be applicable without making Parliament aware? What are
these new schemes? What are the conditions? Nothing is known to the Parliament.
So, that has to be cleared.
Allocation to the various ministries has also come down like anything. So, I
would like to urge upon the hon. Finance Minister one demand. The demand is with
respect to parity in the tax treatment for NRIs, OCIs under the new Capital Gains
Tax Rules for real estate property.
10.02.2025 1169
The last point is regarding the discrimination against the States of Kerala as
well as Tamil Nadu. Sir, even during the disaster period, nothing has been awarded.
So, I totally oppose the Budget proposals for this financial year. It is anti-federal,
anti-people, pro-rich, and pro-corporate.
With these words, I conclude. Thank you very much.

SHRI RAMASAHAYAM RAGHURAM REDDY (KHAMMAM): Sir, I rise to oppose
the Union Budget 2025-26, which was an opportunity to address the pressing
economic challenges facing our country like slowing growth, raising unemployment,
brutal distress and global uncertainties. Instead, what we have received is a Budget
that weakens fiscal federalism, prioritises election-driven announcements over
long-term growth, and once again ignores Telangana development needs.
Despite being one of the fastest-growing States, Telangana contributes only
10.02.2025 1172
three per cent to the population of the country but the GDP is five per cent of the
country's GDP. Telangana has once again been neglected in the budgetary
allegations. The Union Government has strategically reduced our financial
independence, failed to support our key sectors and turned a blind eye to our
needs.
India's projected GDP growth rate of 2024-25 stands at just 6.4 per cent, the
slowest in four years. The reality is that private investment remains weak, rural
consumption is declining and the urban wage growth is sluggish. A Budget should
provide a roadmap for sustainable economic expansion. Instead, what we see is
short-term tax sops and pre-election gimmicks that do little to boost long-term
growth.
Unemployment remains a crisis. The Government may lay claim to a rate of
3.2 per cent, but private estimates put it at 8.05 per cent with youth unemployment
significantly higher. The Budget lacks any major employment generation
programmes.
Telangana, with its robust IT and industrial base was expected to target
initiatives for start-ups, Artificial Intelligence research and MSME growth. Instead,
we received nothing. Interest payments now consume a large part of the revenue
receipts in 2024-25, with no room for productive spending.
In 2023-24, India exported 39 billion USD, while imports were 66 billion USD,
which is a negative trade balance. Even MSMEs, which employ 120 million people,
have seen wage growth of only 13 per cent, while inflation remains at 5.5 per cent,
eroding real income. In fact, 19,828 MSMEs have been shut down during 2023-24.
10.02.2025 1173
The Government speaks of rural prosperity but has ignored Telangana's
pioneering irrigation efforts. There is no support for major projects like the
Palamuru-Rangareddy Lift Irrigation Scheme, despite it being 10 times bigger than
the Koshi Canal project in Bihar that has received funding. The DPR for the project
was submitted in 2022.
The Bayyaram Steel Plant has also not figured in this Budget. There is a
Rs.180 crore pending grant under the AP Reorganisation Act, 2014, which remains
unpaid under various heads. Chilli grown extensively in Telangana, which is one of
the largest consumed spices, does not get a Board but Bihar gets a Makhana
Board. If this Government truly cares for farmers, why is Telangana, one of India's
largest agricultural States, being ignored?
Our request for sanction of 25 lakh houses and 10 lakh houses under the
PMAY and PMAY-G programmes respectively is also ignored. Instead of investing
in self-sufficiency, India remains the world’s largest importer of edible oil,
particularly soybean and palm oil. Seventy per cent of India’s oil is imported. The
Budget fails to provide a strategy to boost domestic oilseed production, hurting our
farmers while increasing import dependence. Import of pulses are all time high in
the last six years, some of which are imported duty free.
Despite the ‘Make in India’ push, India’s manufacturing sector contributes
just 2.6 per cent to global manufacturing output. The Government talks of an
‘Atmanirbhar Bharat’, but where is the actual support for manufacturing hubs like
Telangana? Telangana has been exhibiting a robust economic performance. The
below figures highlight Telangana’s expanding economic base and its significant
10.02.2025 1174
role in contributing to the nation’s tax revenues. As I mentioned, Telangana
constitute only three per cent of India’s population but accounts for five per cent of
India’s GDP. The State collected Rs. 5,211 crore GST in February 2025. This is the
first time Telangana’s monthly collections surpassed Rs. 5,000 crore.
In anticipation of the Union Budget, the Telangana State Government
submitted proposals totalling Rs. 1.63 lakh crore to fund key infrastructure in
development projects. A significant allocation was sought for construction of the
Regional Ring Road to improve connectivity around Hyderabad. This has not
received any funding. For the Hyderabad Metro Rail expansion, funds were
requested to extend the Metro network to accommodate the city’s growing
transportation needs. No funds were considered. For Godavari Drinking Water
Project, Phase II to link Godavari River to Musi River, the State aims to revitalize
the Musi River area enhancing the urban growth and environmental conditions. It
has received no funds. Telangana being an IT Hub for Information Technology
Investment Region, the anticipated funding to develop the ITIR near Hyderabad
intending to boost the State’s IT industry and generate employment has also
received no funds. The National Institute of Design and the National Institute of
Rural Development have also received no funds. For industrial growth and
infrastructure, approximately Rs. 34,000 crore were proposed to boost industrial
development, enhance logistics, and support the growth of pharmaceutical sector.
This was also ignored along with a long list of pending proposals of the Telangana
Government.
The world economy is facing turbulence like trade wars, supply chain
10.02.2025 1175
disruptions, and rising protectionism. But this Budget fails to equip our industries to
tackle these challenges. India’s trade in goods and services as a percentage of
GDP has declined. Instead of boosting exports, the Budget focuses on import duty
reductions that benefit select industries while doing little to encourage self-reliance.
Financial inclusion remains a challenge. Despite improvements, a significant
portion of India’s rural population lacks access to formal banking services. There is
no concrete allocation for increasing access to credit for rural entrepreneurs or for
empowering SHGs and small business owners in Telangana.
The Budget continues the trend of discrimination against southern States.
Despite being economic powerhouses, States like Telangana, Tamil Nadu,
Karnataka, and Kerala receive fewer funds compared to politically motivated
allocations for northern States. I am not saying anything against Bihar’s people; I
do not mean anything. Despite lagging behind in revenue generation, Bihar has
received massive Central support, while States like Telangana, which effectively
utilize resources have been ignored.
Chairperson Sir, this Budget is a betrayal of our farmers, our youth, and our
industries. It is a Budget that prioritizes short-term election gains over long- term
national growth. The Congress-led Government in Telangana is working tirelessly
to create jobs, invest in education, and support farmers. But we need the Centre to
be a partner, not an obstacle. The Congress Party stands for real development –
development that empowers States, supports industries, and creates jobs. We
demand that the Government reconsiders its allocations and give Telangana its
rightful share in the national progress.
10.02.2025 1176
Sir, I am reminded of the hon. Minister’s beginning quote: “Desamante matti
kaadoyi, desamante manushuloyi”. It means: “A country is not made of land; a
country is made of its people.” Telangana does not figure there. Her later quote of
a Tamil quote states: “Just as living beings live expecting rains, citizens live
expecting good governance”.
I think we are going through drought. We are not going to receive anything,
and governance has been given a go bye. The State of Telangana seems to have
been given a pass from both these quotes. It has been eight months since the
elections have finished. I think my BJP colleagues, who have been elected, need
to work with us and put aside our political differences and work for the betterment
of Telangana.
How long are we going to be penalised for the misdeeds of earlier
Governments? This is time we put aside our differences and do something for the
Telangana State. The Prime Minister has made a statement that the country is
inclusive. It does not look like it has included anything.
I will close by saying that do not kill the goose that lays the golden egg. I am
talking here about the southern States. Thank you.

DR. D. RAVI KUMAR (VILUPPURAM): Madam Chairperson, Vanakkam. On
behalf of Viduthalai Chiruthaigal Katchi, I oppose this Budget as it is against the
Constitution of India. Constitution of India ensures equality for all of us. This
consists of the economic equality. We say with pride that India has become the fifth
largest economy of the world. This is true. But if we see the per capita GDP, India
ranks ate 140th place among the countries of the world. It shows that there is a big
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
10.02.2025 1179
disparity on the basis of economy in our country. This Budget should try to bridge
the gap created by this disparity. But this Budget induces the economic inequality
in our country. Hon Finance Minister has talked about four engines. Agriculture,
MSME, Investment and Exports. These are the four engines. Agriculture is an
important sector. Around 65 per cent of our people live in rural areas and out of
which 47 per cent are dependent on agriculture. If we see the budget allocation for
agriculture, there is a reduction of Rs 3900 Crore. It means our Finance Minister
has switched off the engine called Agriculture sector. Next is MSME. This MSME
Sector provides ample employment opportunities. But the fund allocation as well
incentives provided to MSMEs have been reduced in this Budget. Reduction in
import duties is directly impacting the MSME sector. Particularly cycle
manufacturing industry, and shoe-making industry which provide employment to so
many people are very much affected due to this. Labourers from Punjab are
engaged to a large extent in this Sector. People related to MSMEs opine that China
is the direct beneficiary because of our present import policies and the recent
reduction made in import duties. The economic disparities are being encouraged in
such a fashion in this Budget. Backward classes for the majority of our population
in our country. If an allocation is to be made for the backward classes, then there
should be a census done on their population. There is a nation-wide demand for a
caste-based census. Rohini Commission set up for this purpose has already
submitted its report and that report is pending for so long. This Rohini Commission
has given a recommendation for identifying Most Backward among the backward
classes and ensuring reservation facilities reach them separately. By implementing
10.02.2025 1180
the recommendations of the Rohini Commission, this Union Government should
provide a separate reservation for the Most Backward communities. Funds
allocated to the minorities-particularly the religious minorities have been reduced
drastically in this Budget. Many MPs stated here that the reservation in education
for minorities has also been reduced. They are being side-lined in the political
arena. The ruling party of the Day, BJP never fields a single candidate belonging
to the Muslim community in the elections. The fund allocated to the minorities have
also seen a reduction year after year gradually. Particularly Fellowships,
Scholarships meant for their education are being reduced in number. This is gross
injustice faced by them. Scheduled Caste is the big community in this country.
During 1979-80s, during the sixth five-year plan a SC Sub-Plan was introduced to
ensure adequate allocation of funds for SCs. This Government has changed the
name of this Scheme. Not only they have changed the Scheme but they have
reduced the allocation meant for SCs. During the introduction of SC Sub-Plan it
was stated that the budget allocation should be at par with the population of SCs in
the country. This Government has made the cut in the fund allocation by 50 per
cent. As per the 2011 Census, SCs constitute 16.6 percentage of our total
population. As a result 8.3 percent of the total Budget allocation should be made
for SCs and their welfare Schemes. But on the contrary only 3 per cent of the total
allocation is made for SCs. Particularly if you see allocation for Post-matric
scholarships for SCs, it has seen gradual reduction. Revolutionary leader Dr. Baba
Saheb Ambedkar fought with the then British Government in the year 1944 to
provide the right of Post-matric Scholarships for SCs. This Government tried to
10.02.2025 1181
scrap this Scheme in the year 2018. Due to stiff opposition from Viduthalai
Chiruthaigal Katchi and other Opposition parties, it was announced to extend for
just 5 years from the year 2019. They said that this Scheme is being extended only
up to 2025-26. They also informed about the allocation of Rs 35000 Crore for this
Scheme. There is a doubt bothering our minds whether this Scheme will be
extended further? As the Economic Survey states that hereafter the Private
Universities, on philanthropic basis, can provide scholarships to students, there is
botheration in our minds whether this Government will scrap this Scheme of
providing scholarships. I urge that Hon Minister concerned should clarify in this
regard. The income limit for Post-matric Scholarships stands at Rs 2.5 lakh per
annum. This limit has not been raised since last 40 to 50 years. This limit should
be raised. There is an income limit of Rs 8 lakh for Economically Weaker Sections
(EWS). Moreover for Overseas Scholarships the limit was also raised to Rs 8 lakh.
It is gross injustice to continue with the income limit of Rs 2.5 lakh for Post Matric
Scholarships. I urge that this limit should be enhanced. I will conclude by stressing
one more point. After this Government came to power, they have continued to levy
surcharge and cess. During the year 2014-15, the tax collection through surcharge
and cess was at 9 per cent. Now it has risen to 14 per cent. AIDC is a new cess
introduced in the current Budget. I wish to say that this is gross injustice and
therefore I strongly oppose this Budget. Thank you, Vanakkam.

KUMARI SUDHA R. (MAYILADUTHURAI): Thank you Madam Chairperson.
This budget is sound and fury signifying nothing. That is the takeaway for
140 crores of Indians in this Budget. For the common man, this is a bitter pill, sugar-
coated with a populist announcement relating to the enhancement of the personal
income tax slab. It is a truly a corporate Government, which hides the devil in the
detail. What all are you are going to sell, hon. Finance minister Nirmala Ji? What
is the meaning of this hidden statement - an asset monetisation plan to be
developed to harvest Rs 10 lakh crore? Already you have sold and leased the State

assets ranging from ports to roads to airports to ... . Now it is clear that you are
going to sell more State assets in the name of 'asset monetisation'. Rs 10 lakh crore
means half the nation’s roads, power plants, ports, mines and railway properties
will now go the crony capitalists close to the BJP and to the Prime Minister. Will this
Government now tell this august House and the people who are the real owners of
the national assets and the list of Government properties that have been identified
for sale? This Government should keep in mind that it is only the custodian, not the
owner of the assets vested under its control and management by the Constitution.
While the legacy of Jawaharlal Nehru Ji, Shri Lal Bahadur Shastri Ji, Shrimati
 Expunged as ordered by the Chair.
10.02.2025 1188
Indira Gandhi Ji, Shri Rajiv Gandhi Ji, Shri Narasimha Rao Ji and Shri Manmohan
Singh Ji was to build, protect, prosper and pass on to the next generation the
national assets and ensure a hallowed space for India in the comity of nations, this
Government is identifying such well entrenched assets and is selling them under
the asset monetisation programmes. It is scary to think about sale of more State
assets to generate Rs 10 lakh crore this year. History will judge you as the greatest
salesperson, hon. Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman Ji for having sold and for
planning to sell national assets.
Your announcement of creation of assets and programmes are mere words,
whereas the sale alone takes place without any qualms. Do not sell India.
Your Budget eloquently mentions agriculture as one of seven engines of
growth. But what have you given for farmers? Absolutely nothing. Farmers affected
by the vagaries of nature will still stare at wipe out for the season with zero crop
insurance as a safety net. Just consider this. In 2017-18, the number of rejected
claims for crop insurance was 92,869. In the next year, 2018-19, the figure more
than doubled to 2.04 lakh. By 2019-20, it was 9.28 lakh, a whopping 900 per cent
increase. During the years from 2021 to 2023, the number of rejections has tripled
if you compare this with the year 2020.
Despite a rise in the number of applications, the overall claims paid through
the Union Government’s crop insurance scheme, the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima
Yojana (PMFBY), went down by 48.77 percent in 2021-22. The Government data
shows this. This is not my data. This is the Government’s data. In 2021-22, Rs
13,728.64 crore were paid against 8.32 crore insurance claims. The amount is
10.02.2025 1189
lower than the Rs 20,425.01 crore paid in 2020-21 for 6.23 crore applications.
According to the PMFBY website, the gross premiums for 2021, for both rabi and
kharif crops, stood at Rs 28,288.31 crore. The premiums are shared between the
farmer, the State Government, and the Central Government. But the loss goes to
farmers, profits go to the insurance companies.
What does this Budget have for workers employed under the Mahatma
Gandhi Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme? What is your record on this front?
According to the report of the civil society organisation, ‘Lib Tech India’, over 39
lakh registered workers were deleted from the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural
Employment Guarantee Scheme between April and September 2024. The report
also revealed that more than eight crore workers were removed. It is very shameful.
More than eight crore workers were removed from the MGNREG Scheme
registered under the financial year 2022-2023 and 2023-2024. At least 27.4 per
cent of MGNREGS workers and 4.2 per cent of active workers have been currently
rendered ineligible for Aadhaar based payment system benefits.
Our party leader, Shri Rahul Gandhi rightly said that MGNREG Scheme is a
living monument of our Prime Minister Modiji’s betrayal of rural India. En masse
deletion of names of beneficiaries from the list, non-release of funds, bogus entries
and reduction of wages and the number of working days - these are your
achievements on this revolutionary Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme brought
in by the Congress Government to transform and empower the rural Indians. To
us, it was a very important scheme but you have made it a sham.
Madam, the Finance Minister is saying that 10,000 new medical seats will be
10.02.2025 1190
created across the nation. This announcement is like allowing Ekalavya to learn the
art of archery and then cutting his thumb to ensure that his hard-learned art
becomes useless for him. The National Medical Council has mandated that medical
colleges must adhere to the ratio of 100 MBBS seats for every ten lakh population
in the respective State or Union Territory.
The southern States have already exceeded the limit set by NMC. As of
2021, Tamil Nadu has 11,600 seats for a projected population of 7.68 crores. It has
4,000 more seats which the State should have according to the new NMC
guidelines. Therefore, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Kerala and the Union
Territory of Puducherry cannot either open a new medical college or increase
students’ intake in the existing colleges. The Finance Minister proposes but the
Medical Council disposes. These States have invested money, human resources
and community interests. But their future is affected and they do not stand to gain
even a single seat. Is it fair, Finance Minister?
The World Health Organisation recommends that the doctor-patient ratio
should be 1:1000. But in India, there is only one Government doctor for every
10,189 people. If the NMC is concerned for the poor quality of education due to the
mushroom growth of colleges, the right course would be to inspect those colleges
more frequently and disqualify those colleges which are lacking in standards. You
cannot stop and punish a well-performing State with a robust medical education
and public health care system by dragging it down to the lowest level.
There is a proverb in Tamil, “Oru kannil vennei, oru kannil sunnaambu.” The
meaning is, you are showing partisan approach towards the States, favouring some
10.02.2025 1191
and ignoring others. I would like to say that the eye which is crying would be
affected very much and you will face the consequences.
I request our Finance Minister to read this book and come with a good Budget
next time.

रा(cid:213)(cid:367)ीय छा(cid:253)विृ (cid:176) योजना श(cid:322)ु क(cid:236) । वष(cid:170) 2013 के अदं र आिदवासी समुदाय के 20 ब(cid:184)च े जा रह े

(cid:321)पये िमल रही है, उसको 600 (cid:321)पये िकया जाए और 100 िदन(cid:332) का जो रोजगार है, उसे 200 िदन(cid:332) का

LIVE TELECAST OF PROCEEDINGS OF LOK SABHA
Lok Sabha proceedings are being telecast live on Sansad T.V. Channel. Live telecast begins at 11
A.M. everyday the Lok Sabha sits, till the adjournment of the House.
